content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Jury Service | Judicial Branch of California

  - url: https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service

  - source: Judicial Branch of California

  - domain: courts.ca.gov


  ## Content


  # Jury Service


  California courts use a one-day-or-one-trial jury service system. A person who reports
  for jury service is generally not required to come to court for more than one day
  unless assigned to a courtroom for jury selection or selected for a trial. If selected
  to serve, service continues until the trial is complete.


  Prospective jurors must follow the directions on the jury summons. If a person is
  qualified to serve, the summons will explain whether the person must call in, check
  reporting instructions, or report to court. Additional information may be provided
  when the person reports for service.


  ## Failure to Appear


  A person must report for jury service if the person is qualified and has not been
  excused or had service postponed. A person who fails to respond may be fined up
  to $1,500, incarcerated, or both. Jurors should carefully follow the instructions
  on the summons and contact the court if they need help.


  ## Qualifications


  California law says a person is qualified to be a juror if the person is a United
  States citizen, is at least 18 years old, can understand English enough to discuss
  the case, and is a resident of the county that sent the summons. Other disqualifications
  can apply, including certain current criminal supervision statuses, current incarceration,
  current grand jury or trial jury service, or other legal disqualifications. A person
  who is qualified should follow the summons directions and report or call in as instructed.


  ## Excuses, Hardship, and Postponement


  Even when a person is qualified, the person may have an undue hardship that prevents
  service. Examples may include a serious transportation issue, excessive travel distance,
  physical or mental impairment, dependent-care responsibilities, or extreme financial
  burden. A person with an undue hardship may be able to request an excuse or ask
  to postpone service.


  The summons response form and local jury office instructions tell jurors how to
  request an excuse or postponement. Even after asking for an excuse, a juror may
  still be required to come to court to speak with a judge. If a juror is sick or
  injured, the juror may postpone service or request an excuse. A person with a disability
  may request accommodations or, in some circumstances, a medical excuse. Jurors should
  contact the local jury office promptly when they need help with postponement, excuse,
  disability accommodation, or special circumstances.


  ## Reporting and Current Instructions


  Jury-service obligations are tied to the current summons and instructions from the
  court. Jurors who are qualified and not excused or postponed must report, call,
  or respond as directed. If a juror has an approved postponement or excuse, the juror
  should follow the updated instructions provided by the court or local jury office
  rather than assuming that earlier reporting instructions still apply.


  ## Other Practical Information


  Jurors should check the summons or contact the local jury office for transportation,
  courthouse security, dress, courtroom conduct, and special accommodation information.
  Many courts have security screening at courthouse entrances, and some items may
  not be allowed. Employers must allow employees time off for jury service, and employees
  should notify employers as soon as possible after receiving a summons.

  '

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Jury Service Frequently Asked Questions | Superior Court of California
  - County of San Diego

  - url: https://www.sdcourt.ca.gov/sdcourt/jury2/juryfaq

  - source: Superior Court of California - County of San Diego

  - domain: sdcourt.ca.gov


  ## Content


  # Jury Service Frequently Asked Questions


  ## How long will I be required to serve as a juror?


  Jurors are summoned for one day or one trial. Most jury trials last three to seven
  days, but some last longer. If assigned to a case, a juror must serve until the
  trial is completed. If a juror is not selected for a trial, service is generally
  completed in one day. After completing jury duty, a juror is generally exempt from
  jury service for one year. A person who served as a sworn juror on a trial and receives
  another summons within three years may request to be excused.


  ## May I postpone my jury service to a more convenient time?


  A juror may postpone service for up to six months from the reporting date. To seek
  a postponement, the juror may use the online Juror Portal or call the court if still
  eligible to postpone. The juror must have the juror ID number to access records.
  Summoned jurors at the Central Courthouse may also be permitted to report for jury
  duty up to two weeks before or two weeks after the summoned reporting date, as long
  as jurors are being called in for duty.


  Jurors should review telephone standby or reporting information to confirm whether
  jurors have been ordered to report in person. If jurors have not been called to
  report in person, walk-in jury service may not be available.


  ## May I request an excuse from jury service?


  Excuse requests are considered individually. The request must explain why a postponement
  of jury service will not solve the problem. A juror seeking to be excused may use
  the online Juror Portal. The court only notifies a juror if the excuse is not granted.
  Excusal requests are not accepted over the phone.


  ## What if I do not hear from the court about my request?


  The court states that it does not notify people who are excused or disqualified,
  and only notifies people whose excuse is not acceptable under law. A person who
  does not hear from the court may consider themself excused, may call approximately
  two weeks after the request, or may check status through the online Juror Portal.


  If a juror requests a postponement by phone or through the online Juror Portal,
  the juror chooses a date convenient for service. The court will mail a new summons
  approximately one month before the new date.


  ## Students, employment, and practical issues


  Students are not exempt from jury duty, but they may request postponement to the
  next school break. Employers are not required by law to compensate employees on
  jury duty, although many do. Minor hardship or inconvenience to an employer is not
  a legal reason to be excused, but postponement to a more convenient time may be
  requested. If service would create an extreme financial hardship, a juror may use
  the online Juror Portal to request an excuse and provide the information requested
  by the court.


  The court may provide time records or work certificates for employers. Jurors with
  disabilities may request accommodations through the court''s listed accommodation
  process; the accommodation process should not be used to request excusal, disqualification,
  or postponement.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Jury Service Frequently Asked Questions | Superior Court of California - County
  of San Diego
url: https://www.sdcourt.ca.gov/sdcourt/jury2/juryfaq

Your record shows:

- **Original service date:** July 2, 2026  
- **Postponement:** **Approved June 24, 2026**  
- **Current service date:** **August 18, 2026**  
- **Court:** Superior Court of California, County of San Diego  

So, if you missed **July 2**, that date appears to have been replaced by the approved postponement. You should follow the instructions for **August 18**, not the original printed date. Check your updated summons or the San Diego **Juror Portal** for whether you must report in person, call standby, or check online.

If you also missed **August 18**, contact the San Diego County jury office **immediately**—do not simply wait for another notice. Explain that your postponement was approved and ask whether you need to report, reschedule, or take any corrective step. Keep proof of the postponement approval and note the date, time, and person you contact.

California courts warn that failing to respond or appear when required can potentially result in a fine of up to **$1,500, incarceration, or both**, but the court—not an automated record—determines whether consequences apply. The California Judicial Branch says jurors should contact the local jury office promptly if they need help.

Official pages:

- [California Judicial Branch — Jury Service](https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service)
- [San Diego Superior Court — Jury FAQs](https://www.sdcourt.ca.gov/sdcourt/jury2/juryfaq)
- [San Diego Juror Portal](https://jury.sdcourt.ca.gov/)

final_answer:
Your record shows:

- **Original service date:** July 2, 2026  
- **Postponement:** **Approved June 24, 2026**  
- **Current service date:** **August 18, 2026**  
- **Court:** Superior Court of California, County of San Diego  

So, if you missed **July 2**, that date appears to have been replaced by the approved postponement. You should follow the instructions for **August 18**, not the original printed date. Check your updated summons or the San Diego **Juror Portal** for whether you must report in person, call standby, or check online.

If you also missed **August 18**, contact the San Diego County jury office **immediately**—do not simply wait for another notice. Explain that your postponement was approved and ask whether you need to report, reschedule, or take any corrective step. Keep proof of the postponement approval and note the date, time, and person you contact.

California courts warn that failing to respond or appear when required can potentially result in a fine of up to **$1,500, incarceration, or both**, but the court—not an automated record—determines whether consequences apply. The California Judicial Branch says jurors should contact the local jury office promptly if they need help.

Official pages:

- [California Judicial Branch — Jury Service](https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service)
- [San Diego Superior Court — Jury FAQs](https://www.sdcourt.ca.gov/sdcourt/jury2/juryfaq)
- [San Diego Juror Portal](https://jury.sdcourt.ca.gov/)
